title: Find all nodes that are part of any cycle (directed).
description: Find all nodes that are part of any cycle (directed).

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Start with node 0. DFS from 0: 0→1→2→0 (back to start). Found cycle, add 0 to result.
Step 2: Node 1. DFS from 1: 1→2→0→1 (back to start). Add 1.
Step 3: Node 2. DFS from 2: 2→0→1→2 (back to start). Add 2.
Step 4: Node 3. DFS from 3: 3 has no outgoing edges, no cycle. Result: {0,1,2}.

Step 1: Node 0. DFS from 0: 0→1→2→1 (never returns to 0). No cycle for 0.
Step 2: Node 1. DFS from 1: 1→2→1 (back to start). Add 1.
Step 3: Node 2. DFS from 2: 2→1→2 (back to start). Add 2.
Result: {1,2}.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^2) with DFS
approaches.summary: For each node, perform a DFS to check if it can reach itself, marking nodes visited to detect cycles. This is straightforward but inefficient for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Nodes in Any Cycle (Directed Graph) using Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete solution that checks each node for cycle membership using DFS from that node.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force O(n²) approach to find all nodes that are part of any cycle in a directed graph. The intuition is straightforward: a node is in a cycle if it can reach itself through some path. For each node in the graph, we perform a DFS starting from that node to see if we can return to it. If we can, then that node is part of a cycle. We need to be careful about visited tracking - we need a fresh visited set for each starting node's DFS to avoid interference between different searches. The algorithm works as follows: 1) Initialize an empty result set. 2) For each node in the graph: a) Create a fresh visited set. b) Perform DFS from the current node. c) If during DFS we encounter the starting node again (other than as the initial call), add it to result set. 3) Return the result set. Time complexity is O(n²) where n is number of nodes because in worst case we perform DFS from each node, and each DFS might visit all nodes. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and visited sets. This connects to L1 by using the DFS subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include: using a single global visited set (which would incorrectly mark nodes as visited across different DFS calls), not handling self-loops properly, and forgetting to exclude the initial node from cycle detection. The brute force nature makes this approach simple to understand but inefficient for large graphs.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with node 0. DFS from 0: 0→1→2→0 (back to start). Found cycle, add 0 to result.
Step 2: Node 1. DFS from 1: 1→2→0→1 (back to start). Add 1.
Step 3: Node 2. DFS from 2: 2→0→1→2 (back to start). Add 2.
Step 4: Node 3. DFS from 3: 3 has no outgoing edges, no cycle. Result: {0,1,2}.

Step 1: Node 0. DFS from 0: 0→1→2→1 (never returns to 0). No cycle for 0.
Step 2: Node 1. DFS from 1: 1→2→1 (back to start). Add 1.
Step 3: Node 2. DFS from 2: 2→1→2 (back to start). Add 2.
Result: {1,2}.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS to Check if Node Can Reach Itself
ladders.desc: DFS function that determines if a starting node can reach itself through any path.
ladders.explanation: This is the core DFS subroutine that checks whether a given starting node can reach itself through any directed path (excluding the trivial case of being at the starting point). The intuition is: if during DFS from node 'start', we encounter 'start' again (not as the initial call), then there exists a cycle containing 'start'. We perform DFS with backtracking: from the current node, we explore all neighbors recursively. We maintain a visited set to avoid infinite loops in cyclic graphs. The algorithm: 1) Input: graph adjacency list, starting node, current node, visited set. 2) Base case: if current node is start AND we're not in the initial call, return True (cycle found). 3) Mark current node as visited. 4) For each neighbor of current node: if neighbor not visited, recursively call DFS. If any returns True, propagate True upward. 5) Return False if no cycle found. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n) in worst case as it might visit all nodes. Space is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 by being called for each node in the graph. Common mistakes: forgetting to pass the start parameter through recursive calls, not handling the initial call specially (we shouldn't return True immediately when current==start on first call), and not using visited set correctly. The visited set should be specific to each DFS call from a starting node.

examples.trace:
DFS(start=0, current=0, visited={}): Initial call, current==start but it's initial, so continue. Mark 0 visited: {0}. Explore neighbor 1.
DFS(start=0, current=1, visited={0}): Mark 1 visited: {0,1}. Explore neighbor 2.
DFS(start=0, current=2, visited={0,1}): Mark 2 visited: {0,1,2}. Explore neighbor 0.
DFS(start=0, current=0, visited={0,1,2}): current==start AND not initial call → return True. Propagates up: True.

DFS(start=0, current=0, visited={}): Initial call, continue. Mark 0 visited: {0}. Explore neighbor 1.
DFS(start=0, current=1, visited={0}): Mark 1 visited: {0,1}. Explore neighbor 2.
DFS(start=0, current=2, visited={0,1}): Mark 2 visited: {0,1,2}. No neighbors. Return False. Propagates up: False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Cycles via Reachability to Starting Node
ladders.desc: The central insight that a node is in a cycle if it can reach itself.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logical insight that makes the brute force approach work: a node is part of a cycle if and only if there exists a directed path from that node back to itself. This is a fundamental property of directed graphs. The intuition: if node A can reach itself through some path A→...→A (with at least one intermediate node, or via a self-loop), then by definition A is in a cycle. Conversely, if A is in a cycle, then by following the cycle, A can reach itself. This reduces the problem to checking reachability from each node to itself. The algorithm at this level is conceptual: for each node v, check if v is reachable from v via a non-trivial path. We implement this by doing a graph search (DFS) from v, looking for v in the search (excluding the starting point). Time complexity for checking one node is O(n) in worst case. Space is O(n) for search data structures. This connects to L1 by providing the logical foundation for the DFS subroutine. Common mistakes: confusing this with undirected graph cycle detection (which is different), not considering self-loops as cycles, and misunderstanding 'reachability' in directed vs undirected contexts. This insight is what allows us to use simple graph search algorithms to solve the cycle detection problem.

examples.trace: Check if 0 can reach itself: Path exists: 0→1→2→0. Therefore 0 is in a cycle.
Check if 3 can reach itself: From 3, there are no outgoing edges. No path from 3 to 3 exists (except trivial). Therefore 3 is not in a cycle.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with DFS and Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Implementing DFS on a directed graph with proper visited tracking.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental graph traversal technique needed: Depth-First Search (DFS) on a directed graph with visited tracking to avoid reprocessing nodes. The intuition: DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, which is perfect for checking reachability. We need to track visited nodes to prevent infinite loops in cyclic graphs and to avoid redundant work. The algorithm: 1) Represent graph as adjacency list. 2) Define DFS function that takes current node and visited set. 3) Mark current node as visited. 4) For each neighbor of current node: if not visited, recursively call DFS on neighbor. 5) Continue until all reachable nodes are visited. Time complexity is O(V+E) for traversing the entire graph. Space is O(V) for recursion stack and visited set. This connects to L2 by providing the mechanism to check reachability. Common mistakes: using BFS instead of DFS (both work but DFS is more natural for this problem), forgetting to mark nodes as visited before recursive calls (leading to infinite recursion), not handling disconnected graphs properly, and using a list instead of set for visited (making lookups O(n)). This building block is essential because the cycle detection algorithm relies on being able to traverse the graph from any starting point efficiently.

examples.trace:
DFS(0, visited={}): Mark 0 visited: {0}. Explore neighbor 1.
DFS(1, visited={0}): Mark 1 visited: {0,1}. Explore neighbor 2.
DFS(2, visited={0,1}): Mark 2 visited: {0,1,2}. Explore neighbor 3.
DFS(3, visited={0,1,2}): Mark 3 visited: {0,1,2,3}. No neighbors. Backtrack. Done.

DFS(0, visited={}): Mark 0 visited: {0}. Explore neighbor 1.
DFS(1, visited={0}): Mark 1 visited: {0,1}. Explore neighbor 2.
DFS(2, visited={0,1}): Mark 2 visited: {0,1,2}. Explore neighbor 0 (already visited, skip). Backtrack. Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Representing and Accessing Graph Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Creating and using adjacency list representation for directed graphs.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental data structure operation: representing a directed graph as an adjacency list and accessing neighbors of a node. The intuition: an adjacency list is efficient for graph algorithms because it allows quick access to all neighbors of a node. For a directed graph, each edge (u→v) means v is a neighbor of u, but not necessarily vice versa. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty list/dictionary of size n (number of nodes). 2) For each edge (u,v), append v to the list for node u. 3) To access neighbors of node u, simply retrieve graph[u]. Time complexity for building is O(V+E), for accessing neighbors is O(1) average. Space is O(V+E). This connects to L3 by providing the graph representation needed for DFS. Common mistakes: using adjacency matrix (wastes space for sparse graphs), forgetting that directed edges are one-way, initializing the adjacency list incorrectly (e.g., not as a list of lists), and not handling nodes with no edges (should have empty neighbor lists). This basic operation is crucial because all graph algorithms depend on how we represent and access the graph structure.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize list for 3 nodes: [[], [], []].
Step 2: Add edge 0->1: append 1 to list[0]: [[1], [], []].
Step 3: Add edge 1->2: append 2 to list[1]: [[1], [2], []].
Step 4: Access neighbors of node 0: graph[0] = [1].
Step 5: Access neighbors of node 2: graph[2] = [].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Directed Graphs and Cycles
ladders.desc: Basic concepts of directed graphs and what constitutes a cycle.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the basic concepts needed: what is a directed graph and what is a cycle in a directed graph. A directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and directed edges (arrows) where each edge goes from one node to another. A cycle in a directed graph is a path that starts and ends at the same node, following the direction of edges, with at least one edge (allowing self-loops where a node points to itself). The intuition: cycles represent circular dependencies or infinite loops. For example, if A points to B and B points back to A, that's a 2-node cycle. A self-loop (A→A) is a 1-node cycle. The key properties: 1) All edges in the cycle follow their direction. 2) No node appears twice except the start/end node. 3) The path has length ≥ 1. This connects to L4 by providing the semantic meaning of the graph structure. Common misunderstandings: confusing directed cycles with undirected cycles (where edge direction doesn't matter), thinking that any path that revisits a node is a cycle (it must return to start specifically), and not recognizing self-loops as valid cycles. This foundation is essential because the entire problem revolves around identifying these structures in directed graphs.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at A. Follow edge to B.
Step 2: From B, follow edge to C.
Step 3: From C, follow edge back to A.
Step 4: We've returned to starting node A, following directed edges. This is a cycle containing A, B, C.

Step 1: Start at X. Can go to Y, then to Z.
Step 2: From Z, no outgoing edges.
Step 3: Cannot return to X. No cycle exists.
Step 4: Check other starting points: from Y: Y→Z, stop. From Z: no edges. No cycle.

approaches.name: DFS with Color States O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a three-color system (unvisited, visiting, visited) during a single DFS traversal to detect cycles and track nodes in cycles. This efficiently identifies all cycle nodes in one pass.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Cycle Nodes in Directed Graph
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DFS with three-color states to identify all nodes in cycles
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding all nodes that participate in any cycle within a directed graph. The DFS with three-color states approach provides an elegant O(n) solution by tracking node states during traversal. We maintain three states: WHITE (unvisited), GRAY (currently in recursion stack/visiting), and BLACK (fully processed). The intuition is that if we encounter a GRAY node during DFS, we've found a cycle. By carefully tracking when nodes enter and exit the recursion stack, we can mark all nodes in cycles. The algorithm performs a DFS from each unvisited node. When we detect a cycle (encounter a GRAY node), we backtrack through the recursion stack to mark all nodes in that cycle. We also handle multiple cycles and ensure nodes in different cycles are all captured. The time complexity is O(V+E) where V is vertices and E is edges, as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the color arrays, recursion stack, and result storage. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete DFS traversal with cycle detection logic. Common mistakes include not properly resetting cycle tracking when backtracking, incorrectly handling multiple cycles from the same node, and forgetting to mark nodes as BLACK after processing. The key insight is that GRAY nodes represent the current path, so encountering one means we've looped back to a node in our current traversal path.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize colors: all WHITE (0). Step 2: Start DFS from node 0. Color 0 GRAY. Step 3: From 0, visit neighbor 1. Color 1 GRAY. Step 4: From 1, visit neighbor 2. Color 2 GRAY. Step 5: From 2, visit neighbor 0. Node 0 is GRAY → cycle detected! Step 6: Backtrack marking nodes: 2 in cycle, 1 in cycle, 0 in cycle. Step 7: Color nodes BLACK after marking. All nodes are in the cycle.

Step 1: Initialize colors: all WHITE. Step 2: Start DFS from node 0. Color 0 GRAY. Step 3: From 0, visit neighbor 1. Color 1 GRAY. Step 4: From 1, visit neighbor 2. Color 2 GRAY. Step 5: Node 2 has no neighbors → color 2 BLACK. Step 6: Backtrack from 1: all neighbors processed → color 1 BLACK. Step 7: From 0, visit neighbor 2 (already BLACK) → continue. Step 8: All neighbors processed → color 0 BLACK. Step 9: No cycles detected → return empty list.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: The DFS function that detects cycles using three-color states
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core DFS traversal with cycle detection logic. The function takes a node as input and performs depth-first search while maintaining three states: WHITE (0 = unvisited), GRAY (1 = currently in recursion stack), and BLACK (2 = fully processed). The intuition is that by coloring nodes GRAY when we enter them and BLACK when we leave, we can detect cycles when we encounter a GRAY node during traversal—this means we've returned to a node that's still in our current path. The algorithm: 1. Mark current node as GRAY. 2. For each neighbor: if WHITE, recursively call DFS; if GRAY, cycle detected—mark nodes in cycle; if BLACK, skip. 3. After processing all neighbors, mark node as BLACK. The time complexity for this function is O(V+E) in total across all calls, but for a single call it's proportional to the subtree. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion depth and color storage. This connects to L0 by being the main recursive function called for each node. Common mistakes include not checking for GRAY nodes before recursion, incorrectly updating colors, and infinite recursion in cyclic graphs. The key insight is that the GRAY state represents the current traversal path, so any GRAY neighbor indicates a back edge forming a cycle.

examples.trace: Step 1: Color node 0 GRAY. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1 (WHITE). Step 3: Recurse to node 1: color GRAY. Step 4: Visit neighbor 2 (WHITE). Step 5: Recurse to node 2: color GRAY. Step 6: Visit neighbor 0 (GRAY) → cycle detected! Step 7: Backtrack marking 2, 1, 0 in cycle. Step 8: Color nodes BLACK.

Step 1: Color node 0 GRAY. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1 (WHITE). Step 3: Recurse to node 1: color GRAY. Step 4: Visit neighbor 2 (WHITE). Step 5: Recurse to node 2: color GRAY. Step 6: Node 2 has no neighbors → color BLACK. Step 7: Backtrack to node 1: all neighbors processed → color BLACK. Step 8: Backtrack to node 0: all neighbors processed → color BLACK. No GRAY encounters.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Three-Color State System for Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: Using WHITE/GRAY/BLACK states to track node visitation and detect cycles
ladders.explanation: The three-color state system is the central technique that enables efficient cycle detection in directed graphs. WHITE represents unvisited nodes, GRAY represents nodes currently in the recursion stack (being visited), and BLACK represents fully processed nodes. The intuition is simple yet powerful: if during DFS we encounter a GRAY node, we've found a cycle because we've returned to a node that's still in our current traversal path. This works because in a directed graph, a cycle exists if and only if there's a back edge to an ancestor in the DFS tree. The GRAY state precisely tracks which nodes are ancestors in the current path. Algorithm: 1. Initialize all nodes as WHITE. 2. When entering a node in DFS, mark it GRAY. 3. When leaving a node after processing all neighbors, mark it BLACK. 4. During neighbor traversal, if we find a GRAY neighbor → cycle detected. Time complexity for state management is O(1) per node/edge operation. Space complexity is O(n) for the color array. This connects to L1 as the fundamental mechanism within the DFS function. Common mistakes include using only two colors (visited/unvisited), which can't distinguish between fully processed nodes and nodes in current path, leading to false cycle detection in DAGs. The key insight is that the GRAY state creates a 'memory' of the current traversal path without needing explicit path storage.

examples.trace: Step 1: Node 0: WHITE→GRAY. Step 2: Node 1: WHITE→GRAY. Step 3: Node 2: WHITE→GRAY. Step 4: From 2, neighbor 0 is GRAY → cycle! Step 5: Node 2: GRAY→BLACK. Step 6: Node 1: GRAY→BLACK. Step 7: Node 0: GRAY→BLACK.
Step 1: Node 0: WHITE→GRAY. Step 2: Node 1: WHITE→GRAY. Step 3: Node 2: WHITE→GRAY→BLACK. Step 4: Node 1: GRAY→BLACK. Step 5: Node 0: GRAY→BLACK. No GRAY neighbor encounters.

ladders.title: Building Block — Depth-First Search (DFS) on Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Implementing DFS traversal for directed graph adjacency lists
ladders.explanation: Depth-First Search is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For directed graphs represented as adjacency lists, DFS starts at a node, visits its neighbors recursively, and uses a visited set to avoid reprocessing nodes. The intuition is to mimic exploring a maze by always taking the first available path until reaching a dead end, then backtracking. Algorithm: 1. Initialize visited set. 2. Define recursive DFS function: mark node visited, process node, for each neighbor not visited, call DFS recursively. 3. Call DFS from each unvisited node. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited set and recursion stack. This connects to L2 as the traversal framework that the three-color system enhances for cycle detection. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs (need to start DFS from each unvisited node), infinite recursion in cyclic graphs without visited checks, and stack overflow for deep graphs. The key insight is that DFS naturally explores paths completely before backtracking, making it ideal for detecting cycles when enhanced with state tracking.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1 (unvisited). Step 3: Recurse to 1, mark visited. Step 4: Visit neighbor 3 (unvisited). Step 5: Recurse to 3, mark visited. Step 6: Visit neighbor 4 (unvisited). Step 7: Recurse to 4, mark visited. Step 8: Backtrack to 3, all neighbors done. Step 9: Backtrack to 1, all neighbors done. Step 10: Backtrack to 0, visit neighbor 2 (unvisited). Step 11: Recurse to 2, mark visited. Step 12: Backtrack to 0, all neighbors done.

Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1 (unvisited). Step 3: Recurse to 1, mark visited. Step 4: Visit neighbor 2 (unvisited). Step 5: Recurse to 2, mark visited. Step 6: Visit neighbor 0 (visited) → skip. Step 7: Backtrack to 2, all neighbors done. Step 8: Backtrack to 1, all neighbors done. Step 9: Backtrack to 0, all neighbors done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Creating and working with adjacency list representation of directed graphs
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list is a fundamental way to represent directed graphs where each node has a list of its outgoing neighbors. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V+E)) and allows quick access to a node's neighbors. The intuition is simple: graph[i] contains all nodes that have an edge from i to them. For example, if graph[0] = [1, 2], there are edges 0→1 and 0→2. Algorithm to create: 1. Initialize empty list of size n. 2. For each edge (u, v), append v to graph[u]. To traverse: iterate through graph[node] to get all neighbors. Time complexity for accessing neighbors is O(degree(node)). Space complexity is O(V+E). This connects to L3 as the data structure DFS operates on. Common mistakes include using adjacency matrix for sparse graphs (wastes space), confusing directed/undirected representation (for undirected, add both directions), and index errors. The key insight is that adjacency lists naturally represent the 'outgoing' relationship in directed graphs, which is exactly what DFS needs to follow edges.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[], [], []]. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): append 1 to graph[0] → [[1], [], []]. Step 3: Add edge (1,2): append 2 to graph[1] → [[1], [2], []]. Step 4: Add edge (2,0): append 0 to graph[2] → [[1], [2], [0]].

Step 1: Initialize graph = [[], [], [], []]. Step 2: Add (0,1): graph[0] = [1]. Step 3: Add (0,2): graph[0] = [1,2]. Step 4: Add (1,3): graph[1] = [3]. Step 5: Add (2,3): graph[2] = [3]. Step 6: graph[3] remains [].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs and Cycles
ladders.desc: Understanding directed graphs, edges, and what constitutes a cycle
ladders.explanation: A directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) connected by directed edges (arrows), where each edge has a direction from one node to another. A cycle in a directed graph is a path that starts and ends at the same node, following the direction of edges. For example, if there's an edge A→B, B→C, and C→A, that forms a cycle A→B→C→A. The intuition is simple: if you can start at a node and follow arrows to eventually return to it, you've found a cycle. Key concepts: 1. Nodes/vertices: the entities in the graph. 2. Directed edges: connections with direction (u→v means from u to v). 3. Path: sequence of nodes where each consecutive pair has an edge. 4. Cycle: path of length ≥1 where first and last nodes are the same. Time/space considerations aren't primary here—this is about conceptual understanding. This connects to L4 as the fundamental structure we're representing. Common mistakes include confusing directed with undirected graphs, thinking self-loops aren't cycles (they are: A→A), and missing that cycles must follow edge directions. The key insight is that in directed graphs, A→B and B→A are different edges, and a cycle requires following the arrow directions all the way back to the start.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 0. Step 2: Follow edge to node 1. Step 3: Follow edge to node 2. Step 4: Follow edge back to node 0. Step 5: We returned to start → cycle! The cycle is 0→1→2→0.
Step 1: Start at node 0. Step 2: Follow edge to node 1. Step 3: Follow edge to node 2. Step 4: No edge from 2 back to 0 or 1. Step 5: Cannot return to start → no cycle.

approaches.name: Kosaraju's SCC Algorithm O(n)
approaches.summary: Find strongly connected components (SCCs) using Kosaraju's algorithm; all nodes in SCCs with more than one node or self-loops are part of cycles. This method leverages graph structure for cycle detection.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Nodes in Cycles Using Kosaraju's SCC Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to identify all nodes that belong to any cycle in a directed graph using Kosaraju's SCC algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This solution leverages Kosaraju's algorithm to find strongly connected components (SCCs) in a directed graph. The core insight is that any node belonging to an SCC with more than one node or a self-loop is part of at least one cycle. Kosaraju's algorithm works in two main phases: first, it performs a DFS traversal to record finishing times of nodes; second, it traverses the transposed graph in reverse finishing time order to identify SCCs. The algorithm has O(V+E) time complexity and O(V+E) space complexity, making it efficient for cycle detection. The connection to cycle detection is straightforward: in directed graphs, every non-trivial SCC (size > 1) contains cycles, and nodes in trivial SCCs (size = 1) are only part of cycles if they have self-loops. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs properly, forgetting to transpose the graph correctly, or misidentifying self-loops as cycles. The real code pattern involves building adjacency lists, performing two DFS passes, and collecting nodes from non-trivial SCCs.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[2,3], 2:[0], 3:[]}. Step 2: First DFS to get finishing order: Start at 0, visit 1, then 2 (back to 0 completes cycle), then 3. Order stack: [3,2,1,0]. Step 3: Transpose graph: {0:[2], 1:[0], 2:[1], 3:[1]}. Step 4: Second DFS in order [0,1,2,3]: Start with 0, visit 2, then 1 → SCC {0,1,2} size 3 > 1 → nodes 0,1,2 in cycle. Node 3 alone → not in cycle. Output: [0,1,2].

Step 1: Adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[1]}. Step 2: First DFS: Start 0→1→2→1 (back edge), stack: [2,1,0]. Step 3: Transpose: {0:[], 1:[0,2], 2:[1]}. Step 4: Second DFS in order [0,1,2]: Start 0 → SCC {0} size 1, no self-loop → not in cycle. Start 1, visit 2 → SCC {1,2} size 2 > 1 → nodes 1,2 in cycle. Output: [1,2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Finishing Times
ladders.desc: Perform DFS on a directed graph to record nodes in order of finishing times, crucial for Kosaraju's first phase.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine involves a depth-first search (DFS) traversal that records nodes in a stack based on their finishing times (when all descendants are fully explored). The intuition is that by processing nodes in reverse finishing order during the second phase, we ensure we start DFS in the transposed graph from 'sink' SCCs, which helps correctly identify SCCs. The algorithm uses a visited set to avoid revisiting nodes and a stack to push nodes after their DFS completes. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once; space is O(V) for the stack and visited set. This feeds into the full problem by providing the order for the second DFS pass. Common mistakes include not marking nodes as visited before recursion, which can cause infinite loops, or incorrect stack ordering. The real code pattern uses recursion or iteration with explicit stacks.

examples.trace: Start DFS at 0: mark visited, go to 1, mark visited, go to 2, mark visited, 2 has edge to 0 (visited) → finish 2 → push 2 to stack. Back to 1: finish 1 → push 1. Back to 0: finish 0 → push 0. Stack: [2,1,0].
Start at 0→1→2→3, finish 3 → push 3, finish 2 → push 2, finish 1 → push 1, finish 0 → push 0. Stack: [3,2,1,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identifying Strongly Connected Components (SCCs)
ladders.desc: Use DFS on transposed graph in reverse finishing order to group nodes into SCCs.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves performing a second DFS on the transposed graph (all edges reversed), processing nodes in the order of decreasing finishing times from the first DFS. The intuition is that in the transposed graph, SCCs remain the same, but by starting from nodes that finished last (sinks in original graph), we can isolate entire SCCs in single DFS calls. The algorithm uses a visited set and collects nodes reachable from each start node into an SCC list. Time and space are O(V+E). This feeds into the full problem by providing SCCs, which are then analyzed for cycles. Common mistakes include not transposing the graph correctly or using the wrong order. The real code pattern involves iterating over the stack from first DFS and running DFS on transposed adjacency.

examples.trace: Start with node 0 from stack: DFS on transposed from 0 visits 2 and 1 → SCC {0,1,2}. Mark visited. Next node 1 visited, skip. Node 2 visited, skip. Node 3 not visited: DFS from 3 visits only 3 → SCC {3}.
Start with node 0: DFS from 0 visits only 0 → SCC {0}. Next node 1 not visited: DFS from 1 visits 2 → SCC {1,2}. Node 2 visited, skip.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Transposition
ladders.desc: Create a new graph where all edge directions are reversed, essential for Kosaraju's second phase.
ladders.explanation: Graph transposition involves creating a new graph where for every edge u->v in the original graph, we add an edge v->u in the transposed graph. The intuition is that transposing preserves SCCs but reverses reachability relationships, which helps in isolating SCCs during the second DFS. The algorithm iterates over all edges in the original adjacency list and builds a new adjacency list for the transposed graph. Time complexity is O(V+E) to traverse all edges; space is O(V+E) for the new graph. This feeds into the core logic by providing the input for SCC identification. Common mistakes include not initializing empty lists for all nodes or missing edges. The real code pattern uses a dictionary or list of lists.

examples.trace: Edge 0->1: add 1->0 to transposed. Edge 0->2: add 2->0. Edge 1->2: add 2->1. Edge 2->0: add 0->2. Ensure all nodes 0,1,2 have lists: transposed {0:[2], 1:[0], 2:[0,1]}.
Edge 0->1: add 1->0. Edge 1->0: add 0->1. Edge 2->3: add 3->2. Edge 3->2: add 2->3. Transposed is same due to symmetry.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) on a Graph
ladders.desc: Traverse a graph using DFS to visit all connected nodes from a start node.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. The intuition is to mimic exploring a maze by going deep first, using a stack (implicit via recursion or explicit) to track the path. The algorithm marks nodes as visited to avoid cycles and processes each node and edge once. Time complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency list; space is O(V) for visited set and recursion stack. This feeds into building blocks as it's used in both phases of Kosaraju's algorithm. Common mistakes include infinite recursion in cyclic graphs without visited checks, or stack overflow in deep graphs. The real code pattern uses a recursive function or iterative stack.

examples.trace: Start at 0, mark visited. Go to neighbor 1, mark visited. From 1 go to 3, mark visited. Backtrack to 0, go to neighbor 2, mark visited. From 2 go to 3 (already visited). DFS order: 0→1→3→2.
Start at 0, mark visited. Go to 1, mark visited. From 1 go to 0 (already visited). Backtrack. Visited: 0,1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs and Adjacency List Representation
ladders.desc: Understand directed graphs and how to represent them using adjacency lists.
ladders.explanation: A directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges with direction, where an edge from node u to node v means u points to v. Adjacency list representation stores for each node a list of its outgoing neighbors, making it efficient for sparse graphs. The intuition is that this structure allows quick access to all edges from a node, which is essential for graph algorithms like DFS. The algorithm involves creating a dictionary or list where each key/index is a node, and the value is a list of nodes it points to. Time to build is O(V+E); space is O(V+E). This feeds into basic operations as it provides the input for DFS. Common mistakes include not handling nodes with no outgoing edges (empty lists) or incorrect indexing. The real code pattern uses a list of lists or dict with lists.

examples.trace: Initialize lists for nodes 0,1,2,3. Add edge 0->1: append 1 to list of 0. Add 0->2: append 2 to list of 0. Add 1->3: append 3 to list of 1. Nodes 2 and 3 have no outgoing edges, so empty lists.
Initialize for nodes 0 and 1. Add 0->1: list of 0 gets 1. Add 1->0: list of 1 gets 0.
